text.title: Clean Architecture Antipatterns
text.summary: Two critical violations: business logic in controllers, and ORM/framework imports inside domain entities.

antipattern.description: Controller method contains business logic (email uniqueness check, direct entity construction) instead of delegating to a use case.
antipattern.reason: Logic in controllers cannot be reused across delivery mechanisms (CLI, event handler, gRPC). Testing requires a running HTTP server. The same rule ends up duplicated in multiple endpoints.

example.code: @router.post("/users") async def create_user(request: CreateUserRequest, session=Depends(get_session)): if await session.execute(select(User).where(User.email == request.email)).scalar(): raise HTTPException(400, "Email exists") user = User(name=request.name, email=request.email) session.add(user) await session.commit() return user

antipattern.description: Domain entity inherits from SQLAlchemy declarative base or imports ORM columns directly.
antipattern.reason: The domain entity becomes tied to a specific ORM. Unit tests require a database. Migrating to a different database or ORM forces changes to business rules.

example.code: @dataclass class User: # Pure Python, no framework imports email: Email name: str id: UUID = field(default_factory=uuid4) # ORM model lives in infrastructure/persistence/orm/models.py — separate class
